actions.thought: Holding A8o on the button in a heads-up situation is a standard opening hand. I want to build a pot and exert pressure, as Ace-high is often ahead pre-flop. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to take control of the hand and define the range.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) in the Big Blind, a premium hand in Heads-Up play where the Button opens a very wide range. While calling is an option to under-represent my hand, 3-betting is superior to build the pot for value immediately and deny equity to overcards that might appear on the flop. Being Out of Position (OOP), I prefer a larger 3-bet sizing (around 3.5x-4x the open) to charge the opponent effectively. I will raise to 1100 to seize the initiative and extract value.

Facing a 3-bet to 1100 after my initial open to 300, I have A8o on the button. While A8o is a decent hand heads-up, it is not strong enough to 4-bet for value, and folding in position to a single 3-bet is too exploitable. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and see if I can connect with the board or outplay my opponent on later streets. I will call the additional 800 chips.

I hold Pocket Jacks on a Qh-5h-3d board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, the Queen hits my perceived range (AA, KK, QQ, AQ) well, but it is an overcard to my actual hand. Being out of position with a medium-strength hand in a low SPR scenario (SPR ~4), I want to exercise pot control. Betting risks getting raised or called by better hands (QX), while folding out worse hands that I dominate. Checking allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, induce bluffs from missed broadways or draws, and easily call a standard bet. It protects my checking range and simplifies the game tree.

The opponent 3-bet pre-flop and then checked on a Queen-high flop. While I have Ace-high, checking back allows me to maintain pot control and see the turn for free. If the opponent is trapping with a strong range, I avoid losing chips. If the opponent is giving up with hands like AK or AJ, I am still ahead or have a chance to improve. I have the Ah, which gives me some backdoor flush potential.

On the turn, the board is Qh-3d-5h-Tc. The Ten creates some connectivity (straight possibilities for KJ) and hits hands like AT/KT/QT/TT. However, since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is capped; they are unlikely to have a strong Queen or a set of 5s/3s. My hand (Pocket Jacks) is now second pair. Betting here has mixed value: I might get called by a Ten or a smaller pocket pair (88, 99), but I fold out all air and isolate myself against better hands (Qx, TT, AA/KK slow played). Checking is consistent with my flop line, controls the pot size in a bloated 3-bet pot (SPR ~4), and allows the opponent to bluff with missed draws or weak holdings. I intend to call a reasonable bet given the opponent's show of weakness on the flop.

Pro 3-bet pre-flop but has now checked on both the flop and the turn. This sequence often indicates a hand that has missed the board, like AK or AJ, or perhaps a medium pocket pair that is playing cautiously. My Ace-high has some showdown value and I also hold the Ah, which is a useful blocker. By checking back here, I maintain pot control and take a free card to the river, where I might improve or win at showdown without further investment. If Pro is attempting to trap, I avoid falling into it by not betting.

I hold Pocket Jacks on a Q-3-5-T-4 board. The action went check-check on both the flop and the turn. The opponent's double check-back heavily suggests they do not have a strong Queen or a set, as they likely would have bet to protect against flush draws or extract value. Their range is weighted towards underpairs (99, 88, 77, 66), weak pairs (A5, A3), or missed high cards/draws (AK, AJ, flush draws). While I likely have the best hand, betting is thin value because worse hands (like 88) may simply fold, and better hands (random Qx or Tx that checked back) will call. Checking is the superior option here for two reasons: 1) It allows the opponent to bluff with their missed draws or air, which is the only way I get more chips from those hands. 2) It allows me to get to showdown cheaply against their marginal made hands. If I check and they bet, I plan to call given their show of weakness on previous streets.

The river is a 4 of clubs, making the final board Qh 3d 5h Tc 4c. Pro 3-bet pre-flop but has checked every street since. This sequence often indicates a hand with moderate showdown value that is afraid of the board or a completely missed high-card hand like AK or AJ. While my Ace-high is weak, it beats all missed draws and some lower high-card combinations. Since the pot has stayed small and I have not improved, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play. There is little value in bluffing here as Pro may call with any pair given the passive line I have taken.

I hold 3s 4c on the Button (SB). In Heads-Up play, while the Button range is wide, 43o is one of the weakest starting hands (bottom 10%) with poor playability and low equity. With deep stacks (88 BB effective) and it being early in the match, there is no incentive to get involved with trash hands. The standard GTO play is to fold. I will fold and conserve chips.

I am holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand in any poker format, and particularly strong in heads-up play. Being on the button gives me a positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Q8o, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. In heads-up play, Q8o is well within the defending range, ranking roughly in the top 45% of hands. I need to call 200 chips to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. Although I will be out of position post-flop, folding is too tight given the hand strength and stack depth (86bb effective). A 3-bet is unnecessary and turns a marginal value hand into a bluff that plays poorly against a calling range. Therefore, calling is the standard and most profitable play.

On this A-high flop (As 5c 3s), the board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), who contains all the strong Ax hands in their range. As the pre-flop caller out of position with Queen-high and only a backdoor flush draw (Qs), I do not have a range advantage or a hand strong enough to lead (donk bet). My standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Flash checks back, I can try to realize equity or bluff later. If Flash bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, though Q-high is likely a fold against a standard c-bet on this texture.

I have top pair, top kicker on a flop that includes a flush draw and some straight possibilities. Pro checked to me, and I need to bet for value and protection. A bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate here to build the pot while keeping Pro's weaker range in.

I hold Q8o (Qs) on an As 5c 3s board. I checked and the opponent bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). This Ace-high texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. While I hold the Queen of Spades which gives me a backdoor second-nut flush draw, my current hand value is just Queen-high. Calling a half-pot bet out of position with only backdoor equity and Queen-high is generally -EV, as I am drawing nearly dead against any Ace and am behind most pocket pairs or flush draws. Without a pair or a more substantial draw, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am holding Ac 8h on the Button (SB). In a heads-up match, A8 offsuit is a strong starter, well ahead of a random hand. Being in position, I want to take the initiative. Folding is out of the question. While limping is an option, raising puts pressure on the Big Blind and builds the pot with a hand that has decent showdown value and blocker potential. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size to test the opponent's strength and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-7 offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200, which is a min-raise from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, 9-7 offsuit is a standard defending hand against a small raise. I will call and see a flop out of position.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) on a King-high board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play to see how they value their range on this texture. While the board favors the opponent's range, my draw provides enough equity to continue if they bet a reasonable size.

I hold Ace-high (Ac8h) on a Kh-5s-6h board. Having raised pre-flop and been called, the pot is 400. The opponent checked to me. This board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (pairs, straight draws, flush draws), but also favors my range as the pre-flop raiser with strong Kings. However, with Ace-high, I have decent showdown value but little ability to get value from worse hands by betting (worse hands fold, better hands call). Betting creates a scenario where I might get blown off my equity by a check-raise. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially catch a bluff on later streets or get to showdown cheaply. It is a classic pot control spot for a medium-strength showdown hand.

The turn pairs the King, which is a significant card. Since both of us checked the flop, it is less likely that Pro has a King, although they could be checking back a medium-strength hand or a draw. My hand (9-high with a gutshot straight draw to an eight) has very little showdown value. Checking is the best option to see a free river or evaluate a potential bet from the opponent. I will check and see how the action develops.

I hold Ace-high (Ac8h) on a board of Kh-5s-6h-Kd. The action went check-check on the flop, and the opponent has checked to me again on the turn. The second King on the turn reduces the likelihood that the opponent has a King (combinatorics), but since I checked back the flop, my range is also somewhat capped, though I can credibly represent a King or an Ace-high that is pot controlling. Betting here achieves little value; worse hands (like Q-high or missed draws) will fold, and better hands (like any pair of 5s, 6s, or a slow-played King) will call. By checking, I maintain pot control, realize my equity to the river, and keep my showdown value intact against the opponent's bluffs or weaker high cards. I am happy to take a free river card.

The board has paired Kings and three hearts are present. I have 9-high and missed my straight draw. Since the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely the opponent has a King or a very strong hand, but I also have zero showdown value. In this small pot, I will check to see if the opponent is willing to check it down. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have no hand to call with.

I hold Ace-high (Ac8h) on a final board of Kh-5s-6h-Kd-4h. The river brings a third heart, completing a possible flush, and also puts a 4-card straight on the board (4-5-6 with a 7 or 2-3 completing it). The opponent has checked to me for the third time. My line of checking back the flop and turn signifies a pot-control line with marginal showdown value or a give-up. It is difficult for me to credibly represent a strong hand like a King or a Flush after checking twice, so a bluff here is unlikely to fold out a pair (like a 4, 5, or 6) which the opponent likely holds if they are checking down. However, my Ace-high is still ahead of their complete air and missed non-standard draws. Betting offers no value as worse hands fold and better hands call. Checking back ensures I realize my equity and see a showdown for free.

Jack-four offsuit is a weak hand, but being on the button in a heads-up situation, I want to maintain pressure and not give the big blind a free look at the flop. A standard raise to 300 allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand with positional advantage. If faced with a significant re-raise, I can comfortably fold without losing much.

I hold 8d 3c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). This is a very weak hand (offsuit, unconnected, low rank) which plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds, my equity realization will be low against an opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I hold A5o on the Button (SB). In Heads-Up play, this is a strong starter hand that should almost always be played. Being in position, I want to take the initiative. My opponent has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression (Hand #5). A min-raise is a standard, low-risk play to steal the blinds or build a pot in position with a hand that has decent showdown value and nut potential (straights). I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Pro has raised to 200 (a min-raise) from the button. While the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call, 6-2o is extremely difficult to play profitably out of position, as it rarely hits strong pairs or draws and has poor equity realization. Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold the bottom of my range and wait for better spots to engage.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 7s 5d. While this is a weak starting hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression and applying pressure from the button is important. I have previously raised to 300 on my buttons with varying results. I will continue this strategy to see if I can take down the pot pre-flop or navigate the post-flop play if called. If I face a significant re-raise, I will likely fold given the weakness of my hand.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3x BB) from the button in every opportunity so far (Hands 1, 3, 5, and now 7), suggesting an extremely wide and aggressive opening range, possibly near 100%. My hand, Kc2c, is suited and holds approximately 53% equity against a random hand. Although I am out of position, I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 600 total). Even with an equity realization discount for being out of position, K2s is strong enough to defend against such a frequent opener. Folding suited Kings heads-up against a wide range would be too tight.

I hold King-high (Kc2c) on a dry, rainbow Q-6-4 flop. Being out of position against a highly aggressive opponent who raises 100% of buttons, my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. There are no flush draws available, and my straight potential is negligible. Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands or fold out better ones significantly. The standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If they check back, I realize equity for free; if they bet, I can re-evaluate, though typically this would be a check-fold without a specific read or draw.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a relatively dry board (4-Q-6). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the Big Blind checked to me, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately if the opponent missed, or build a larger pot if I hit my straight on the turn or river. Semi-bluffing with 8 outs is a standard and effective play here.

Opponent has a 100% opening frequency on the button and follows up with a standard half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) on a dry Q-6-4 rainbow board. While my K2s has some theoretical equity against a wide range, I hold only King-high with no immediate draws and am out of position. I am well behind any Queen, pair, or Ace-high. The pot odds of 4:1 (calling 300 to win 1200) are decent, but my ability to realize this equity is poor out of position without a strong draw. Floating here would likely lead to facing difficult decisions on future streets. Folding is the prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 3s 9h, which is an extremely weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). Although I am in position post-flop if I play, this hand has very poor playability due to lack of connectivity and low card ranks. My opponent has shown the ability to defend their Big Blind (Hand #4) or fold (Hand #6), but investing chips with 93o is generally negative EV. I will fold this trash hand to preserve my stack.

I have Jack-Six suited on the button, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. I've consistently raised to 300 from the button in previous hands and have seen the opponent fold to pre-flop and flop pressure several times. Raising here maintains my aggressive image and allows me to take control of the pot early.

I hold Ah2d in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. In heads-up play, any Ace is a statistically strong hand often ahead of the Button's wide opening range. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600), requiring only 25% equity, while A2o has significantly more against a standard opening range. While a 3-bet is a valid option to utilize the Ace blocker and fold out better hands, the opponent's larger open size and previous willingness to call 3-bets suggests calling is the lower variance and more standard line to defend my big blind without bloating the pot out of position.

I am out of position with Ace-high on a K-T-4 board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kings, Tens, and broadway combinations. My hand has very little equity to improve (only a runner-runner straight draw). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold, but if it checks through, I retain some showdown value with Ace-high.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has come out King-high with a Ten. Although I haven't connected with this board, my opponent has checked to me. In previous hands (Hand 3 and Hand 7), the opponent folded to a 300-chip continuation bet on the flop. Given my table image and the opponent's tendency to fold to post-flop pressure when they don't have a strong hand, I will place a standard-sized C-bet of 300 to take down the pot now.

I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit on a King-Ten-Four board with two spades. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has continuation bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half pot). This board texture strongly favors the Button's opening range, which contains many Kings, Tens, and broadway draws. My hand has very little equity—I have only Ace-high, no flush draw, and practically no straight potential. While I get 3:1 odds, calling out of position with just Ace-high against a range that connects well with this board is negative EV. The opponent has shown a tendency to c-bet, and without a pair or a strong draw, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips.

I hold A9s on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. My previous button strategy has involved min-raising with playable hands (like Hand 4 and Hand 6), which has successfully either taken the pot pre-flop or allowed me to play in position. Raising allows me to build the pot with a value hand and seize the initiative. I will raise to 200 (min-raise).

In heads-up play, defending the big blind against a min-raise with a hand like Q7o is standard due to the excellent pot odds (4:1). While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the hand has enough high-card value and playability to see a flop rather than folding. A 3-bet is not ideal here as the hand isn't strong enough for value and there are better candidates for bluffs.

The board [8h, Kc, Kh] is paired and favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. As the Big Blind, checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how the button reacts. I have Queen-high and no direct draw, so I am looking to see a free turn or evaluate based on the opponent's bet sizing.

I hold As9s on a board of 8h-Kc-Kh. This is a paired board texture. In a previous similar hand (Hand #4), I checked back Ace-high on a K-high paired board and saw a cheap showdown. My Ace-high has good showdown value here against the Big Blind's wide range, but if I bet, I primarily get called by hands that beat me (Kx, 8x, pocket pairs) or strong draws. Betting folds out hands I am already crushing. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and keep my opponent's range wide, potentially inducing bluffs on later streets or realizing my equity for free. I will check back to see the turn.

The board is 8h-Kc-Kh-2c. After Pro checked back on the flop, it is less likely they hold a King. However, my Queen-high still has some showdown value against total air, and betting into a paired board that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range can be risky without a solid draw or value hand. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or observe Pro's action on the turn.

I hold As9s on a board of 8h Kc Kh 2c. After checking back the flop, the turn card is a brick (2c) that introduces a second flush draw but doesn't connect with many logical hands. My Ace-high still likely has the best hand against the Big Blind's checking range, which contains many missed floats and air. Betting here folds out hands I am beating (random queens/jacks/tens high) and only gets action from pairs or strong draws. By checking, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and get closer to a cheap showdown where my Ace-high is often good. This line is consistent with my play in Hand #4 where I successfully realized equity on a paired board.

The board has been checked through the flop and the turn. My Queen-high has some showdown value in a pot that has been played this passively. Pro's checks suggest they likely do not have a King or a strong Eight. While I could attempt a bluff to push off a weak pair or a better high card, checking is the most prudent play to reach a showdown for free, as I may still have the best hand against Jack-high or worse.

I hold As9s. The board is 8h Kc Kh 2c 9h. The river card gives me two pair (Kings and Nines) but also completes a possible heart flush. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. The opponent's range likely consists of missed draws, weak pairs (like 8x), or perhaps a slow-played monster/flush. Betting here is thin for value; worse hands (like 8x) are unlikely to call on this scary board (paired K, possible flush), and better hands (flush, Kx) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value without risking chips against a flush or trapped trips. I likely have the best hand against his checking range, so I will take the free showdown.

Holding 8s 5h on the button, I will continue my strategy of applying pre-flop pressure. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to continuation bets on the flop in Hands 3, 7, and 9. Even though 85o is a weak hand, the positional advantage and the opponent's recent 'fit-or-fold' post-flop behavior make a standard raise to 300 chips a viable play to take down the blinds or set up a profitable c-bet opportunity.

I hold 8c 2c in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in the deck. The pot odds are 33%, but my raw equity is barely that, and my realized equity will be much lower playing out of position against an opponent who c-bets frequently (as seen in Hands #3, #7, #9). Calling here would likely result in folding to a c-bet on most flops, leading to a chip bleed. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct play.

Current hand is T4o. In heads-up play, while the button range should be wide, T4o falls into the bottom tier of hands (approx bottom 15-20%). The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently (calling 2 out of 3 times against my opens in previous hands). Even though I would have position post-flop, playing a weak hand like T4o against a sticky opponent is not profitable. Folding is consistent with my previous play where I folded 43o and 93o.

I have King-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy so far has involved raising to 300 from the button, and I will continue this sizing to keep my range balanced while building a pot with a strong hand.

I am in the Big Blind with [Ad 3d] facing a button raise to 300 from Flash. A3 suited is a strong heads-up hand with approximately 58% equity against a random hand. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot of 400, offering 2:1). While a 3-bet is a viable option due to the ace blocker, calling is standard to keep the pot manageable and realize equity with a suited ace, especially since I am out of position. Flash has shown a tendency to c-bet frequently, so I can re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Ad3d) on a relatively dry board of [3h 9c 7s]. In Heads-Up play, being out of position (Big Blind), it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Flash has demonstrated a high c-bet frequency in previous hands (Hands #3, #7, #9), so checking allows me to induce a bluff or call a bet with a reasonable bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air range and only get action from better hands (9x, 7x, pocket pairs). Therefore, checking is the most +EV decision to realize my equity.

The board of 3h-9c-7s is relatively dry and favors my pre-flop raising range. I have two overcards (King and Queen) which provide decent equity. Given that Pro has folded to several continuation bets in previous hands (Hands 3, 7, and 9), a standard half-pot bet of 300 is likely to take the pot down now or set up a profitable situation on later streets.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Ad3d) on a 3h-9c-7s board. Flash has bet 300 (half pot). Given the Heads-Up context and Flash's history of frequent c-betting (seen in hands #3, #7, #9), his range likely includes many missed overcards and draws that I am currently ahead of. I have decent showdown value and getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win 900), I only need 25% equity. My hand beats all his air bluffs (like KQ, QJ, JT) and has 5 outs against top pair. Folding here would be too tight against an aggressive opener. Raising would likely only get called by better hands. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play to keep his bluffing range wide.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Ad3d) on a board of 3h-9c-7s-5h. Being out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown a tendency to bet frequently, leading out (donk betting) is not optimal. Betting would likely fold out the hands I am beating (air/bluffs) and isolate me against better hands (9x, 7x, 5x, overpairs). Checking is the standard line to control the pot size, induce further bluffs from the opponent, or hopefully get to a cheap showdown. The turn card connects with some straights (68) and two-pair combos, reinforcing the need for caution.

On the turn 5h, the board becomes more coordinated, offering potential straight and flush draws. Pro called my flop continuation bet, which is a departure from their previous behavior of folding to flop pressure. This suggests Pro likely has a pair or a significant draw. With King-high and two overcards, I have some showdown value against their draws and equity to improve on the river. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see a free river card, where I can reassess the situation based on the final board texture and Pro's action.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Ad3d) on a board of 3h-9c-7s-5h-2s. The action went check-call on the flop and check-check on the turn. Flash's turn check-back suggests he has a capped range, likely consisting of marginal made hands (weak pairs) or give-ups with high cards. Betting the river is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like K-high) and will only be called by better hands (5x, 7x, 9x). Checking is the best option to realize my showdown value cheaply. If he checks back, I have a decent chance of winning against his missed broadways. If he bets, I can evaluate, but my primary goal is a cheap showdown.

On the river, the board has not changed significantly with the deuce of spades. Pro's decision to check for a third time suggests a marginal hand that is likely looking to get to a cheap showdown. My King-high has very little showdown value, but Pro's call on the flop indicates they likely have at least a small pair (like a 3, 5, or 7). Since I checked the turn, I've signaled weakness, and betting now would be a pure bluff. Given the tournament context and Pro's previous calling behavior on this hand, it is unlikely a bet would force a fold from a better hand, so I will check and take my chance at showdown.

Current hand is 6c 2c, which is a very weak suited connector (bottom 10% of hands). In previous hands from the Button/SB, I have either folded junk hands (Hand 2, 8, 12) or min-raised playable hands (Hand 4, 6, 10). The opponent 'Flash' has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind by calling min-raises (Hand 4, 10), which reduces the effectiveness of a steal attempt with such weak holdings. Playing a bloated pot post-flop with 6-high is not profitable. Therefore, folding is the most prudent action to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have been aggressive in previous hands and the opponent has shown a tendency to fold, 5-3 offsuit is generally too weak to justify a raise or a call, especially when out of position post-flop (though technically in position heads-up, the hand strength is still insufficient). I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

In the Small Blind with K5o, I have a hand that is well within the standard opening range for Heads-Up play. The opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind but plays passively post-flop, often checking down. Raising to 200 allows me to seize the initiative, potentially take the pot down pre-flop, or play a pot in position with a decent high-card hand. I prefer raising over limping to define ranges and apply pressure.

I am in the big blind with 83o. The button, Pro, has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. Although the pot odds are 3:1, 83o is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. I have been playing relatively aggressively in previous hands, and I don't need to defend this particular weak hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While this is a weak hand, in heads-up play it is standard to open a wide range of hands from the small blind to maintain pressure and utilize positional advantage. I have consistently raised to 300 in previous hands from this position, and I will continue that pattern here to avoid being predictable and to potentially take down the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o, a very weak hand. The opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb). Flash has shown a consistent pattern of opening to 3x from the button in previous hands, suggesting a relatively wide range, but Q2o plays poorly post-flop out of position. I am getting 2:1 pot odds, requiring ~33% equity, which Q2o might technically have against a very wide range, but realizability is low due to reverse implied odds (domination on Queen pairs) and lack of connectivity. Against a 3x size, I should defend tighter than against a min-raise. Therefore, folding is the prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 4h 6c, which is a very weak offsuit hand (bottom 10-15% of hands).
